speaker: interviewer
text: The survivor's name is George Steinberg. The interviewer is [? Donna ?] [? Kenny. ?] And we're in Long Meadow, Mass. Continue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Steinberg, you were describing for us the second gentile family that hid you. And would you please spell that man's name-- Ivan Lishvwicz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ivan-- no. Lishvwicz. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Ivan Lishvwicz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lishvwicz, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lishvwicz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will try. Maybe not correct, but I will try. Ivan, I mean, you know. And Lishvwicz, L-Y-- C-- I-- S-H-- right, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe it's not correct, the spelling. Not maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure not. But I correct my [INAUDIBLE], [? what ?] [? so now ?] [? I can ?] [? think. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then, after eight months in hiding with your wife and son--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at this man's--

speaker: interviewee
text: This man.

speaker: interviewer
text: --farm, you had to leave because his wife got scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, his wife, she was scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next? Where was the next place that you hid?

speaker: interviewee
text: At night, I went out. And I know-- maybe it's not necessary, some certain Jewish people were killed on the way, when I went. We stopped there, by the grave. You know that? Maybe it's not necessary there. And we started to pray and ask them, they should help us, and we went further. And I went with the same story, to another guy. The same-- the same, of course, [? as some ?] what those [? are. ?] But the German's a policeman. And so he helped me in that time. And I used the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they know us. It was a farmer. And they know we were five brothers too. You know? And I mentioned the same thing, like, my brother's alive in case [? I ?] [? got ?] [? mine, ?] [? different, ?] he will be afraid for my brothers. But nobody from my brothers [? were ?] sleepy kids. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Business, anyway. And with the same story, and I came to him. And somehow, I make sure to relay the story because-- and I came with the same mystery. And I saw him. He was scared to see me. Because they were afraid. It was certain. You know, and when I start to explain that I'm alive and he knows me, and I have my brothers. I mention the brothers, in case, before I ask him my favor, what I want from him. But I still mention to him already, well, yes, I am alive and my brother's alive. Only to put it in his mind, in case to change nature of-- maybe he's got some imagination. And I-- and I went to his wife and he helped me. Because his son was also a policeman for the Ukrainian police or the Germans. And then-- and then in the front, the front-- the war came in the nearer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody knows, already, the Germans start to lose the war. It was easy for him to put it in his mind, maybe I can help him, too. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they decided. And I was there. And I did the same-- the same old because I had, already plans from before, what's helped me up till now. And I dig a hole. And I was there the rest-- until I was liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your wife, and your son, and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my wife and my sister. My sister left three-- maybe a week before because she was alone. And we already know some Partisans was in the city from where I came. And she left us and went herself. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: She left about a week before you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe, not even a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So altogether, you were in hiding for about 26 months or so?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, something-- yeah about--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years, that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- in my head, it was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you and your family eat, when you were in hiding for over two years?

speaker: interviewee
text: How I eat?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find food?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the farmers gave us a little bit, food. And I went to-- oh. Every time it becomes a different history, which is connected to the-- to this history. I find food here and there. And-- and steal from-- from the pigs, whatever they're given. You know, some potatoes. And [? bones ?] from cherries in the tree. You knocked with the teeth and gave it to the child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cherry pits?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [? that's it. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Cherry pits?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, you ask me and I make it shorter, the story. Because I took a long time. It was connected to this thing. And in my mind, when you ask for the questions, it's connected to my mind. and I should tell it to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- yes, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe it's not exactly what-- you know, what-- I-- I-- exactly I mean, to make it shorter or to tell you the story. The-- the-- the main-- the main thing, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: You would leave the holes at night--

speaker: interviewee
text: At night.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and go into the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the hole-- of course, only at night. But the question, with a favor to ask him. [? They-- ?] because like the second guy, what you have the name, told me right away in daytime. And I start to asking and cry for [? you, ?] [? leave ?] [? me. ?] I survived at night. And I [INAUDIBLE] and they give me a permit. I went out at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet other people in the woods at night? Were there other people hiding and moving in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. At that time we were by ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you go into the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the woods is not so--

speaker: interviewer
text: To find food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Find-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Food. And I went to the-- when I went from the holes, I didn't went through the woods. I went only because through the woods, I went to another house, which was one house from the other one, 10 kilometer. You see, and that's where I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'd go-- OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, ask me. Maybe I'll correct something there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your-- did your wife and son ever leave? And-- and your sister, did they ever leaves the holes? Never?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. Only me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every day and every night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day, all the day and night. And every-- this-- nine months in one place, eight or nine months in the other place. No. That's it. I went out sometime at night to help my-- this was what, the second one, to help to go to the woods, steal some wood for the-- for him to heat in the hole. But not ever-- my wife was not never in the-- in the woods. In the hole.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult to keep a growing little boy down in the hole?

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, I've got a kid, you know. And he used to lay late because it was dark with no light, and in a hole. And he used to cut straw. And he said he makes food for the-- for the little calves. You know, a kid, my good kid. I'm saying now, not to give him the credit, but a good kid. If he would be a bad kid, we wouldn't survive. God helped us, if you are-- believe in God. And everything was, according to time and the situation. Even in this situation, he was a good kid, not a crybaby.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep warm, all of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the hole, it was warm. Under the cows, warm. This was this--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the winter too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The-- the clothes, what we had, was rotten, [? full. ?] Because what I took my wife, she made from a sack, a skirt. And that were the-- the clothes. Because no, they became rotten. We didn't became rotten. The clothes became rotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you leave the hole to go to the bathroom? Or would you go to the bathroom in the hole?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, in the hole. This was-- [INAUDIBLE]. That's in the hole. And at night, we took it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have water to drink?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our-- in the same, like, another part like this. Also not-- not too much water to drink, should we have to go to find water and to bring the water, only as much as possible. And that time, the judgement from ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you were afraid that German soldiers would find you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every second, not every minute. Every second. I was in the hole and the Germans were on the top. They came to take away a cow from my farmer. And we shake hand to each other and squeeze the hand to say goodbye to each other. Because if he is on the top, he will find out us. And hear somebody's underneath. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were all holding hands underneath because you thought the soldier would find you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. Scared. Like the saying over here, when they talk, scared to death. And that's why they're scared. That death was before our eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about, can you describe for me about how big the hole was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately. I didn't measure. We were four people in the hole. Only on in a side, not comfortable to lay on the side. If somebody turned, then all of us have to turn to correct ourselves. Yes. And this is not make-up story. That's what it was. You know, I kept going to the top with cows. And on the cows, wood. And the wood was not [? real ?] [? straight ?] wood. It was-- the cows could get-- fall-- fall in with us too. But not happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: It never happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never happened. Yes, not happen this. Like a cow, she made wet. And the wetness came through this hole, yes. This lots of times, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: The cow would urinate and it would come through into the hole?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to stand in this hole?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not to stand-- to stand up, not straight. Like this, not only me, all of us. This is not a make-up story. This is a story from our life, past life. And that's what it is. Maybe I'm not telling you exactly like it was. Because it was more scary in that time, like the story what I'm telling you now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of you ever get sick in those over two years that you are in hiding in the holes?

speaker: interviewee
text: By a miracle, and my wife was a more believer than I, that God gave us the sickness was my wife when I went out and start [INAUDIBLE], [? the ?] [? burn ?] from the damp, [? in ?] the back, she couldn't go out. This was the sickness to my wife. And but also by a miracle, my sister took [? her ?] [? out ?] [? and then we ?] [? went ?] [? out. ?] [? And ?] [? then ?] [? she's fine. ?] Our life was abnormal thing to remember and to explain the way, what it was, this life. I was no life. And even animals couldn't survive this type of situation, likely. But if you are a believer, or a believer-- if God will, he can do everything. We survived by miracle, even now, you know. And we didn't forget. And I didn't forget. And my little boy--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you pray?

speaker: interviewee
text: To tell you the truth, not so. Yes, with what? Oy, God, we always was. My wife was bigger believer we are. She always talked to God. And another thing, she's more believer. And she came from off in a religious family, her brother was-- it's not necessary to mention-- was a rabbi. And you know, more religion she had than was mine. And she-- after when we had discussions sometime, here God helped us with the survive. Because she was more believer than I. I was never-- I don't know if you call a believer or fanatic-- a normal man. And even now, I'm not over-- I go to synagogue. I belong to the synagogue. You know already. I did for the synagogue, [INAUDIBLE], in my-- imagine what [? I weared. ?] But not all for nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the woods at night, the night that you were traveling to the third farm, you said you came upon a grave.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were dead Jews. And you stopped and you prayed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, 12 Jews. I remember the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know them?

speaker: interviewee
text: --the amount. I didn't-- some-- if I knew them? Of course. Of course I knew them. But maybe I wouldn't correct you the names now, because there was 12 there. And I know where they are, because they are Ukrainian men. A matter of fact, he was-- he had only one leg, a cripple. And he took him also because they paid them at that time. And then he gave him out. Because you know, we thought they'd take him for a month or for two months. They took his and they gave them out, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were 12 people in the grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: 12 people in the grave, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: One man had only one leg. One of the men had only one leg, in the grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know this is the-- the owner from the house, what he accepted, had one leg. The men, yeah, they were Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these 12 people people who had been in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They lived in hiding. They went and they-- they ask him. Maybe they paid him. Because details, I don't know how he took them in, for what reason. But I know one of them, they killed him. I heard already, a the woman's-- when this guy was in another people, they killed him and they shoot him there. They didn't took him in [? the ?] [? same. ?] They shoot him there and there and-- a matter of fact, when we are went at night-- maybe I mentioned already-- with-- to the tour guide, we stopped for the grave. I know where the grave is. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do-- how did the Germans find the 12 people that they killed in the grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the Germans, Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewee
text: If the Germans will-- only the Germans, we would-- we would have, maybe, 20% from the-- from the population survived. I will say 20%. I guess, no, Ukrainians [? there, ?] because there it was a poor area. And well, the people, farmers, they had six children, 30 acres of land. And all the six children-- I'll give you, for example, how they survived. And their life was a poor life. And when you're poor, your nature is different. You look for any benefit you can get. And the-- that-- the time and the season came for them, this benefit, to kill a Jew and to have whatever he got in the pockets, or the somewhere. This is truth. It's not make-up story. Politics, this what is-- this was the life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- the Ukrainians killed these Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh yeah, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to take whatever money they had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and whatever valuables they had.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, whatever. Oh yeah. Oh, it was a lot of folks. I tell you only what I know. I know I don't know everything. Because at that time, a lot of them from the area spread all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you and your family were in hiding--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were you were afraid, also, of Ukrainians? Of course, for any live human, doesn't matter who. Any human alive, we are afraid. Not afraid for animals or for something. Only because the population became animals. This is not a make-up story. That's what the story is. And that's what it is. Maybe I'm not describing everything, every details. Because it was more-- even the details was more certain things-- was more scary and wondering, how could this happen? Was-- because in a normal time, this is not happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the normal time made the Germans. The help, from our area, Ukrainian business and everything. It was Czechs. It was other ones. Ukraine, yes. They was. Because they were poor in their mentality. Because we was-- the whole areas was not educated like over in the United States. Everybody went to school as a child. And if you haven't got education, your feeling is different, like an animal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you explain, again, why you told the three farmers that you have five brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: But the reason, this was my reason, to make him scared, and scared out of their mind to kill us. Because I didn't-- you couldn't exam their nature. If they are good nature or bad nature, I accept only my mind, what my mind dictate me that time, to give him in case he will kill me. Maybe because I will-- I will tell you a story. It's not-- some people, why they survived. And they also paid up, certain people, certain things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to pay these farmers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not pay. I'm not talking money about pays, They paid with their lives too. After.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by mentioning your brothers--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were implying that the brothers might--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in my mind in case they have a bad way of thinking, they should be scared for their lives. Because the time was not a normal time. The way of thinking by people was a different way of thinking. And that's the reason what I-- what I that time, I used my mind, and this object this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't even know, at this time, if your brothers were alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Matter of fact, I-- I know already, about the four brothers, I was sure are they killed. Or they were killed. One brother, I had in mind maybe he survived the same way, because he's still alive. He did survive what which I-- the same way what I did. He's now in Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, please, what happened to your brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers? They're killed and they are-- I-- I mentioned they have in the grave. They are in there, in the grave, in the picture, what I have there, yeah, they're there with my mother. Two brothers, two brothers with mother together in this grave. One brother was hiding also but all the time, comes a different story. And the same subject, yeah, one brother, he was with 12 people who were murdered by Ukrainian farmer. And they gave him out. They killed them, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were betrayed by a Ukrainian farmer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was the farmer?

speaker: interviewee
text: The farmer-- I forgot. Can you believe it? [INAUDIBLE] to say. But one farmer, after I survived, I went to him because he was connected with a-- with the big shot, what I said, I do business with him, to him. And I find my brother's jacket, a winter jacket. I took him away. But I forgot--

speaker: interviewer
text: After you were liberated--

speaker: interviewee
text: After I was liberated, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you saw your brother's jacket on one of the farmers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which brother was that, who was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the smallest brother, what I-- I-- I didn't mention where he was killed. He was killed with 12 people, other one, in a farm, what he was working there for the Germans. It's a-- the history is longer than what to now-- now's time. And it's not exactly I go, you know, word by word, or by [? history ?] [? by history. ?] Came to my mind. And so that's why I'm mixed. I know I didn't share the story exactly like this, you know, because names and-- and other things. But I make up--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know which brother it was who was killed with his 11 friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I know the brother? Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was youngest brother. And the name was Yukel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Chuna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chuna? Chuna was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the-- the Germans killed him on the way. He was in the-- Chuna was in the army, in the Russian army. And then it was-- they didn't took him to be a soldier, only a worker to-- dig dig holes because they were start to fighting with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and-- and they took him in. In the-- what do they call them? In-- in-- [? plan. ?] They-- they caught them with another-- [? I find ?] [? that ?] they killed [? them ?] on the way. We went--

speaker: interviewee
text: He took-- they-- they caught him in the army. It was not a army, a civil army. But the [? Russians, ?] when they took him in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he had a-- up here-- what they call this in-- in English? And you know, to-- to cover up, as he is not a soldier going [? around. ?] And they find him and they killed him along the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because another boy came. And he told me, you know, the name was [? Chuna ?] at that time. They killed your brother. But they had to call me. Another offer came in and said, don't kill him. We need him. Yes. And he was survived. He came. They caught him 14 days later when he came back. And he mentioned the name to somebody, that my brother was killed and that he saw it. It's-- it's a big history. And you couldn't tell the history exactly in order like it was, what came to your mind. And the mind, don't forget, it's 55 years-- 55 years I'm from there. And my mind is not the same. I'm older. And my situation, I explain, is not the same situation what was two, three year-- three years ago. I admit. I know it. I know my failure. But I couldn't help it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your sister, Faiga?

speaker: interviewee
text: Faiga was killed with two children in other city, where she lives. She was married to another city. Faiga was killed with two children and with her husband. And she was hiding by a policeman. A-- it was a Polish policeman. And then they find them there. He didn't give them out. They find them there. Them

speaker: interviewer
text: She was hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hiding. Because she was from another city. Up there they had a ghetto like by us. And then-- and then she find a place. And the same [INAUDIBLE], what I find out.

speaker: interviewer
text: She-- she escaped another ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: She escaped-- not escaped. She left the ghetto and went to [INAUDIBLE], when they start to kill the ghetto. And it was about 40 kilometer from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What ghetto was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Warvek, that time the name from the-- from the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you try to spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-A-R-V-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember about what time or what year she was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The year, I can maybe guess. In '43, yeah, the year maybe. But I couldn't guess. Matter of fact, it's not necessary. I was by her house after I survived. Went by there to look. We still was strong enough to go. But the house was taken by Polish family. That's-- I'm only saying-- this is-- whatever I say, it's not necessary, even today's story, from this. Because took four years, the history [? if we want, ?] it took me 10 years from the history when we started until I came to the United States, a refugee. It took 10 years. Because the history it was started in '39. And I came here '49. And never a normal life, and never in a-- in a house, [? in ?] [? my ?] [? home ?] [? of ?] [? my ?] living, usually. 10 years, it took.

speaker: interviewer
text: And finally, tell me what happened to your brother, Hershel, and to your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: They killed them in ghetto. This is the-- the picture, where they are, with all the people there, where they took--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- what year they kill them? In 1942, then, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who killed them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. The Germans didn't kill. The main thing, the killers, was Ukrainian, most of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your--

speaker: interviewee
text: They belonged to [? militia. ?] But the German was fighters, and they were in the army. And they took helpers when they came. For this-- where you know, most in our area was Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were Hershel and your mother killed inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They took them-- this I heard later. Because we had friends too. And-- and they told me they saw when they-- they took my mother with my-- with other Jews, on-- on trucks. And they brought them to their grave. And that's where they kill-- they killed her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside the ghetto, at a grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside, yes. Did I show you the picture? Yeah, you see the picture. You have to see the picture of the grave. And this is by a miracle. And what-- my mind, not because I want [? the ?] [? carrot. ?] I'm older. I know what's waiting for me tomorrow, will be the same thing as everybody. But my mind was not this way. When I make the picture, when I-- when I survived, I had a mind to do it, and I did  it. And a made [INAUDIBLE] bigger. And I don't want to say-- to tell you what I'm saying. I have in the-- maybe it's necessary, if you and I-- for the history-- in my synagogue where I belong, I made from brass, and I made it in New York. My names for my brothers and sisters from my mother, and the names from my wife's. And then I thought of the name, only mentioned the six million people from brass, and six candles are burning. This I have in-- in the synagogue. And my life-- I-- and I wanted to did whatever a human with a feeling. I didn't lost my feeling, what we lost, and what we went through. And I did-- I don't know if it's helps or doesn't help. But I did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you made a memorial here at the synagogue in Springfield--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's in the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Matter of fact, I added-- because they are building now in the-- over here, you know, a memorial center. And-- and my mind came, the synagogue is not forever. Today it's there. Maybe because he is-- my son is active there too. And he's got-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SOBBING] --a human feeling. And he's active. Wouldn't help us. I know in my mind, I'm not a fanatic. I'm only saying, human thought and human thinking, and that's it. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But he's active. And you'll see, if you might guess when he was here, I'd be surprised. But he's that type of a kid. I have three of them, fine kids. I don't want to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Were Hershel and your mother killed before you escaped the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you escaped?

speaker: interviewee
text: After. After I escaped. They didn't offer me. My sister picked up later too, also, when everybody was killed. I just mentioned it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have that, when we-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry I mix up maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not exactly what, you know. But I tell you this story, and everything keeps-- and sometimes I change the mind. And my mind is not the same too, what I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about when you were liberated and when you came out of hiding, finally.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came out from the-- in 1944, I think, January or February, because it was wintertime. I was liberated and came out from the hole. Then there, from 1944, because the-- the war still was going on, yeah. Even when I was liberated, it's no use even to think-- I had to go because I was younger. And the-- even the-- the-- the Russians start to mobilize with some [? headlock. ?] And they then took me. I was in the-- in the back. And they took me to the army. Because some people, they survived like me. They went to the army. They didn't come back from the army. Survived Hitler, and then came-- because the war was going on still. Because the war was ending in 1945. It was the the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that it was safe to come out of hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, from the hiding? Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know it was safe?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- yeah, this I know because I came out from the hole. And sit on-- on [? a headache. ?] And that, I took already. Because the shooting and some, from the guy who told me already, he knew it. And yes, this I find out. And matter of fact, it was already they call at that time, Partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And some Partisans went by already. The-- the-- the farm where-- where I lived. And you know. And we were waiting for this, and anxious. And this was the time we find out. You know? So some partisans told the farmer where you were in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. They didn't know somebody there. Nobody was trusted at the time. Only we find out it's already Russian Partisans. They would come in there, in the beginning. And to them we are trust because we were waiting for them. Now, some people who came to the Russian, only the Russian. Because in our area, the occupation was from the Russian people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the partisans in the woods? Would you see them in the woods when you would go in there at night for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if-- if I see them?

speaker: interviewer
text: The partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them. I didn't see them. If I see them, they will see me. I was maybe, 10, 20, you know, meter or from them when they went by. And I sit in grass. But they didn't see me. If they see me, I would be underground already. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had heard that the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything is, if you are believer, I'm not so sure for nothing. But if God will, if you believe in God, to survive, he made them sleepy. then I saw them. They didn't see me. It's only by a miracle, if you believe in a miracle. I am not a-- such a strong believer. But on this subject, only in miracles, it's happened to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mr. Steinberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of this tape. And we'll resume in a moment.
